dimensions.summary: The player is a nimble problem solver who treats walls, height, rented tools, and two related kingdoms as a field of route choices rather than a fixed sequence.
After an opening arc, the player chooses a destination, equips the likely tools, traverses one of two connected worlds, completes a dungeon, and reinvests rewards into ownership or upgrades.
Top-down movement and combat are joined by wall merging, lateral movement along surfaces, portal traversal, and a broad tool set regulated by a shared replenishing energy meter.
Completed dungeons, rescued figures, purchased tools, discovered rifts, opened shortcuts, and collectible thresholds persistently change access and available strength.
Hyrule and Lorule form corresponding but nonidentical overworld graphs linked by wall rifts, so a blocked route in one world may be approached through the other.
Most major tools become available through rental early enough to support flexible dungeon order, while ownership, wall merging, rifts, and local keys control finer-grained revisits.
Wall seams, narrow ledges, visible height, tool-shaped affordances, energy limits, and paired-world correspondence provide the evidence used to form and test traversal hypotheses.
Dungeons remain bounded room graphs with a focused interaction grammar, but the broader set can be attempted in multiple orders because required tools are not tied only to prior dungeon completion.
Rifts, caves, minigames, heart pieces, rupees, and hidden collectibles reward both close inspection of familiar terrain and comparison between corresponding world locations.
Rupees fund temporary rentals and permanent purchases; ownership survives defeat and enables collectible-based upgrades, turning economic commitment into reduced future risk.
Combat combines positional swordplay, energy-limited tools, wall evasion, and bosses that test the local dungeon rule without requiring a single global acquisition order.
Ravio converts item access into a shop relationship, while residents, rescued sages, minigame hosts, and collectible-related characters connect optional tasks to growth and world knowledge.
The conflict is revealed through transformations, captures, rescues, and crossings between parallel kingdoms; agency resumes between compact scenes so player-chosen dungeon order remains visible.
The overhead camera exposes room plans while modeled height and wall-side movement reveal vertical relationships; wall merging temporarily reframes Link as a lateral figure on the boundary.
Flattening animation, energy drain and refill, highlighted cracks, rental loss, purchase permanence, stereo depth, and hint ghosts clearly communicate unusual rules and recovery options.
The interface exposes the shared item-energy meter, equipped tools, map links, and rental status, while optional hint glasses exchange system Play Coins for contextual guidance.
Defeat returns rented items to the shop but preserves permanent progress; players can re-rent, buy equipment, select another objective, or use optional hints rather than face a hard dead end.
A 2.5D overhead presentation preserves classic planar readability while stereoscopic height, wall planes, and layered floors make vertical separation an actionable rule.
The original is a single-player Nintendo 3DS title whose stereoscopic display and Play Coin system affect depth reading and optional hint access without requiring networked play.

Implementation requires paired-world mappings, wall-surface navigation, reversible avatar projection, a shared energy economy, flexible dungeon dependencies, rentals, persistent ownership, and hint-state checks.

contributionHypotheses: Temporary capability access can support nonlinear objective order when ownership and failure preserve meaningful economic decisions.
A reversible movement-plane transformation can unify traversal, portals, evasion, and spatial puzzles.
Corresponding worlds create strong exploration reasoning when crossings are sparse edges and local topology differs in consequential ways.
uniqueOrNonportable: The names and depictions of Hyrule, Lorule, Ravio, the sages, wall-painting form, Maiamai, exact rift map, narrative scenes, music, and dialogue are nonportable.
Specific dungeon order, item roster, room layouts, collectible locations, boss weaknesses, and visual transformation sequence remain evidence only.
openQuestions: A later accessibility review should test the game's critical depth cues with stereoscopic display disabled rather than inferring equivalence from the product page.

dimensions.summary: The player performs a growing heroic competence: an urgent nighttime rescue expands into mastery of two related worlds and a broad tool set.
The campaign cycles through overworld investigation, bounded dungeon dependencies, a new capability, a boss resolution, and renewed access or meaning in earlier spaces.
Eight-direction movement, sword attacks, charging and spinning, dashing, lifting, throwing, pulling, swimming, and assignable tools let action and puzzle use share one vocabulary.
Story milestones, rescued characters, opened shortcuts, collected equipment, and transitions between Light and Dark World states persist and alter reachable routes.
A dense continuous overworld uses strong regional landmarks, while two geographically corresponding world states make location memory useful across changed terrain and settlements.
Gloves, flippers, hammer, hookshot, boots, and world-transition access turn visible obstacles into remembered return promises and reveal shortcuts through old regions.
Rooms present switches, floor patterns, movable objects, tool-compatible targets, and multi-room dependencies with feedback that supports local hypothesis and revision.
Multi-floor dungeons combine maps, compasses, small keys, a big-key dependency, a signature item, loops or drops, and a boss that closes the dramatic unit.
Side caves, ledges, cracks, wells, minigames, and remote islands offer health, magic, money, equipment, bottles, or shortcuts, making landmarks carry unresolved possibility.
Heart pieces and containers, sword and armor upgrades, bottles, magic, arrows, bombs, and rupees create several independent but mutually supporting forms of preparedness.
Enemies telegraph movement and defensive rules in a readable top-down plane, while bosses combine positioning with a dungeon tool or recently reinforced combat concept.
Characters provide rescue goals, rumors, services, upgrades, and short transactional side routes whose outcomes can change inventory or world access.
The playable opening uses weather, telepathic appeal, infiltration, and escape to embody the premise before a larger revelation reframes the world and campaign stakes.
Top-down framing exposes room dependencies and surrounding threats; scroll boundaries, stair transitions, floor changes, and world shifts stage larger spatial reveals.
A directed opening layers movement, weapon pickup, combat, switches, carrying, and escort-like escape, while later tools are introduced in spaces that immediately require them.
The status screen assigns one tool while showing equipment and quest progress; dedicated buttons preserve sword, movement, action, and item use as stable roles.
Saved progress and selectable restart regions reduce total repetition, while bottles and fairies give players an optional, discoverable buffer against combat failure.
The 2D top-down view supports dense object interaction and readable combat lanes; floors, pits, stairs, and world swaps represent vertical or parallel structure discretely.
The original Super Famicom design is single-player and uses the expanded controller to separate sword, item, dash, map, pause, and contextual action functions.
A faithful abstraction needs connected overworld regions, paired map states, multi-floor room graphs, persistent gates, inventory and resource systems, scripted openings, and robust return points.

contributionHypotheses: A playable dramatic opening can introduce role, controls, and core loop by assigning each story beat a necessary verb.
Two corresponding world states can deepen topology when state differences create routes and meanings rather than merely replacing visuals.
Optional recovery storage can turn exploration reward into player-controlled accessibility.
uniqueOrNonportable: The named dual worlds, map correspondence, character identities, rescue structure, relics, exact dungeon layouts, audiovisual motifs, and scripted reversal are source-specific expression.
Individual tool-target pairings and boss solutions remain evidence; only the abstract teach-confirm-vary-exam ordering is portable.

dimensions.summary: The player inhabits a self-directed wilderness survivor who reads terrain, weather, materials, creatures, and ruins to choose a personally meaningful route through Hyrule.
The cadence is to sight a landmark, plan an approach, traverse or improvise through local conditions, discover a shrine, quest, resource, or memory, and use that gain to pursue a farther curiosity.

Running, climbing, gliding, swimming, riding, cooking, gathering, stealth, melee, archery, shield use, physics manipulation, elemental interaction, and photography overlap across traversal and combat.

Activated towers and shrines, completed quests, recovered memories, defeated Divine Beasts, opened travel points, weather, time, fire, electricity, and moved objects all change present options at different persistence scales.
A continuous three-dimensional landscape uses visible towers, peaks, settlements, roads, geological barriers, and Hyrule Castle as nested landmarks rather than a mandatory corridor graph.

The plateau teaches required capabilities, after which most gates are soft constraints of stamina, temperature, equipment, knowledge, danger, or chosen preparation rather than a fixed dungeon sequence.
Material properties, environmental reactions, silhouettes, airflow, magnetic highlighting, sound, NPC clues, and shrine geometry let the player form multiple physical hypotheses.
Numerous compact shrines isolate rule experiments, Divine Beasts provide larger reconfigurable challenge spaces, and Hyrule Castle permits several routes through a hostile final landmark.
Curiosity yields shrines, Korok seeds, resources, equipment, memories, quests, vistas, travel points, and knowledge that can be useful even when no permanent item is awarded.
Spirit Orbs, stamina, hearts, inventory slots, armor upgrades, rupees, food, elixirs, arrows, materials, and replaceable weapons form several interacting preparation economies.
Encounters support direct fighting, stealth, terrain, elemental reactions, remote bombs, physics, avoidance, and preparation; bosses foreground readable attacks and champion-related abilities.
Settlements, stables, travelers, researchers, and regional leaders offer rumors, services, contextual tasks, and reactions that turn self-directed travel into local social purpose.
An amnesiac opening aligns player and protagonist knowledge, while optional memories and regional quests reveal the past in a route-dependent order around a stable rescue objective.
A freely controlled third-person camera supports horizon scouting, close combat reading, vertical route inspection, Sheikah Slate aiming, and cinematic reframing during major reveals.

The plateau safely introduces powers and survival constraints, while elemental effects, stamina, noise, temperature, weapon warnings, map marks, and physics responses continuously explain consequences.
Health, stamina, temperature, sound, weather, equipment, runes, map pins, quest records, and inventory surfaces provide layered planning information without prescribing one route.
Frequent saves, fast travel, retreat, cooking, route changes, avoidance, fairies, and later revival effects allow recovery from danger without removing environmental or resource consequences.
The 3D view makes elevation and distant landmarks primary planning data, but requires robust camera control, climbing surfaces, line of sight, streaming, and fall-state readability.
The original is a single-player experience released simultaneously on Switch and Wii U; no cooperative state is required, while controller motion can supplement selected aiming interactions.
gaps: The primary design is single-player and no core causal rule depends on multiplayer, asymmetric screens, networking, or a platform-exclusive input device; platform editions are handled as version deltas.

dimensions.summary: Its principles require streamed open terrain, systemic physics and elements, weather and time, climbable geometry, AI perception, dynamic encounters, layered save state, map discovery, and broad interaction testing.

contributionHypotheses: Distant landmark visibility can generate self-directed objectives when traversal supports several viable approaches.
Systemic environmental rules are most portable when the same material response informs traversal, puzzle solving, and combat.
Optional challenge nodes can reward curiosity with both avatar growth and a denser future travel network.
Narrative fragments can be discovered nonlinearly when each reveal is anchored to a searchable spatial clue.
uniqueOrNonportable: Hyrule's exact terrain, towers, shrines, Divine Beasts, Sheikah technology, characters, creatures, memory scenes, quest text, music, iconography, and visual language are nonportable.
Specific shrine solutions, Korok tasks, boss weaknesses, recipes, item tables, landmark coordinates, and physics set pieces remain evidence only.
The portable value lies in abstract systemic and spatial relationships, not in reproducing this work's recognizable content density or map silhouette.
openQuestions: A later version delta should measure how the Nintendo Switch 2 Edition's performance, additional save slot, and Zelda Notes change navigation and recovery without treating it as a separate work.

dimensions.summary: The player embodies Zelda as a resourceful problem solver who answers danger and blocked routes by learning echoes, recombining their properties, and restoring places damaged by rifts.

The cadence is to observe an object or creature, learn its echo, select and summon a useful copy, combine copies or Sync with the environment, explore rifts, and convert discoveries into a broader solution vocabulary.

Movement and conversation are joined by learning, selecting, summoning, dismissing, and repositioning echoes; Sync and reverse-bond movement manipulate objects, while a limited swordfighter form supplies direct action.

The learned-echo catalogue, Tri's available summoning power, repaired rifts, resolved local requests, collected materials, equipment, and swordfighter resources record progress at different persistence scales.
A compact overhead Hyrule links settlements, fields, waters, deserts, caves, dungeons, and rift entrances to fragmented Still World spaces whose floating terrain remixes recognizable spatial motifs.
Progress is governed by regional rift states, echo knowledge, Tri's summoning capacity, environmental properties, Sync use, and the player's ability to compose a route rather than only by fixed keys.

Object shapes, movement patterns, creature behavior, echo costs, surfaces, hazards, and visible target geometry expose a grammar in which several learned copies can satisfy one spatial or systemic need.
Still World expeditions and conventional dungeons turn the broad echo vocabulary into denser sequences of traversal, switching, combat, and boss tests before a rift or regional condition changes.
Optional routes and local problems yield new echoes, ingredients, equipment, currency, swordfighter upgrade resources, automaton opportunities, and shortcuts that widen future approaches.

Tri's power expands the usable echo budget, while currency, ingredients, smoothies, accessories, and swordfighter upgrades create parallel economies for experimentation, resilience, and direct combat.

Threats can be answered by summoning monster echoes, exploiting environmental objects, using Sync, repositioning allies, or spending limited swordfighter energy, with bosses emphasizing readable states and tool choice.

Residents present concrete worries, services, recipes, rumors, and side requests; helping them directs exploration toward echo applications and can unlock rewards or fabrication options such as automatons.

Zelda's search for missing people and the cause of the rifts progresses through restored regions, rescued figures, companion reactions, and short scenes that convert visible world repair into story advancement.

The elevated 2.5D camera keeps Zelda, Tri, summoned copies, height differences, hazards, and destinations readable, while shifts in framing and depth support rift entrances, reveals, and compact performed moments.

Learn prompts, catalogue entries, placement previews, echo-cost markers, Tri's power display, Sync links, valid positioning, creature behavior, and immediate world reactions explain what each copied element can do.

A growing echo selector, cost indicators, equipment and smoothie menus, map and quest records, swordfighter energy, contextual prompts, and summon replacement controls expose a large but reusable action library.

Summoned copies can be dismissed and replaced without consuming ordinary inventory, failed arrangements can be rebuilt, smoothies support recovery and resistance, and alternate echo combinations reduce dependence on one solution.
The overhead 2.5D presentation preserves top-down route clarity while making elevation, stacking, object volume, water, moving platforms, and the fragmented depth of rift spaces mechanically legible.

The original is a single-player Nintendo Switch game controlled through a conventional gamepad; echo selection, placement, companion behavior, and simulated creatures are local rather than synchronized between players.
gaps: The primary design is a local single-player adventure and its core rules do not depend on multiplayer, network state, asymmetric screens, or a platform-exclusive input device.

dimensions.summary: Its principles require a tagged echo registry, summon-budget accounting, placement and collision checks, allied creature AI, object-binding constraints, layered navigation, persistent rift and quest state, and broad combination testing.

contributionHypotheses: Turning encountered entities into reusable verbs can make exploration, combat, and puzzle growth share one progression language.
A visible summon budget can preserve open-ended composition while keeping local state readable and replaceable.
Externalized creature behavior can shift combat emphasis from repeated direct attacks toward selection, placement, and adaptation.
A reversible binding relationship can support diverse puzzles when control direction and collision consequences are always visible.
uniqueOrNonportable: Zelda, Link, Tri, Hyrule, the Tri Rod, the Still World, named characters, creatures, locations, rift fiction, iconography, dialogue, music, and exact story events are nonportable.
The precise echo roster, costs, object properties, dungeon layouts, boss responses, automata, recipes, and regional restoration sequence remain evidence only.
Portable principles require an original copy vocabulary, companion fiction, visual silhouettes, resource economy, encounter topology, audiovisual identity, and causal story context.
openQuestions: A comparative playtest should measure how selector burden and discoverability change as the echo catalogue grows, especially for players who rely on accessibility support.

dimensions.summary: The player or group commands four coordinated heroes as a small force, alternating solidarity, formation tactics, independent action, and friendly competition.

Enter a course, explore public and private spaces, assign items or formations, solve multi-body obstacles, collect enough Force to empower the shared sword, defeat the course threat, break the final barrier, and receive a performance ranking.

Top-down sword and item actions combine with group, split, leader-swap, carry, and four formations; multiplayer gives individuals direct control while solo play treats the four as a reconfigurable unit.
Course-local enemies, switches, interiors, player positions, held items, collected Force, and the final barrier are synchronized; completion advances through eight regions of three courses each.
Courses alternate shared television spaces with interiors shown privately on connected handhelds, allowing hidden subspaces, simultaneous routes, and reunions without losing the public macro-scene.
Headcount, color, formation, item, public-private position, Force threshold, and course completion gate progress; insufficient Force can send the team back through the current course to search.
Puzzles communicate required formation, number, color, screen layer, direction, or item through object shape and response; group reconfiguration is frequently the answer grammar.
The main adventure uses discrete courses rather than a continuous overworld, each with an internal arc of exploration, puzzles, mass combat, collection, and an end barrier or boss.
Force gems appear from enemies, vegetation, mechanisms, hidden spaces, and cooperative actions, making thorough route coverage both a score opportunity and a clearance requirement.

Force is a course-local empowerment and gate resource, while item ownership is largely situational; stage results compare individual contribution rather than building a conventional persistent wallet economy.
Large enemy groups make formation shape meaningful, while bosses and barriers test coordinated force, item use, and the ability to regroup after independent movement.

Authored NPC interaction is secondary to human coordination; multiplayer participants help or compete, while solo leader switching and formation control substitute a command relationship for peer negotiation.
Episodic course framing, massed hero motion, transitions between public and private screens, barrier destruction, and end-course rankings deliver the primary theatrical rhythm.
A shared television camera frames public action, personal handheld screens frame interiors or hidden activity, and formation silhouettes keep the four-body unit readable during crowded scenes.
Formation icons, immediate block and enemy reactions, visible Force accumulation, screen transfer, regroup commands, and course-end scoring teach through repeated local consequences.
Solo play can use a GameCube controller, while one-to-four-player modes use linked Game Boy Advance systems; leader, formation, item, Force, and screen location require clear mode indicators.
Course segmentation and local regrouping limit replay distance after mistakes, and solo support avoids mandatory partners; multiplayer still requires specialized handhelds and cables.
Top-down 2D supports precise formation footprints and dense enemy fields, while the split public-private display model turns screen ownership into a spatial rule.
One-to-four players are supported; handhelds plus GameCube link cables provide private views, while solo play can command all four heroes with formations from a standard controller.

A faithful abstraction needs synchronized multi-display world state, public-private visibility rules, four-agent formation and regroup logic, course-local resource accounting, scalable player ownership, and stage-result scoring.

contributionHypotheses: A party can become a reconfigurable spatial tool when formation changes alter footprint, force, and coverage under one input grammar.
Public and private synchronized views can turn visibility boundaries into cooperative level mechanics.
A course-wide aggregate collection threshold may reward optional engagement while preserving route flexibility.
uniqueOrNonportable: The exact four-hero mythology, named sword and Force resource, characters, regions, story incidents, formations' branded names, and course sequence are recognizable authored surface content and must not be reused.
Specific room layouts, screen-transition scenes, bosses, ranking categories, item roster, and audiovisual identity are nonportable.
The GameCube-to-GBA cable implementation is a historical platform expression; only abstract public-private synchronized views may be compared.
openQuestions: A primary-play trace should distinguish which public-private effects are mandatory in solo controller play versus handheld play.
Exact course recovery, individual scoring weights, and Force retention rules need formal state traces before pattern tests encode them.

dimensions.summary: Each participant is one member of a duplicated hero team that must coordinate to advance while still competing to be the most successful collector.
Two to four players enter a themed area, split up or regroup, exchange access to tools, solve simultaneous-action obstacles, collect rupees, defeat a boss, and compare individual results.

Top-down swordplay, carrying or throwing teammates, pulling and pushing together, using a currently held tool, separating, regrouping, and opportunistic collection make social coordination part of the action set.
Stage-local switches, defeated enemies, collected rupees, tool availability, and player positions form a shared synchronized state; major progress is organized by completed areas and earned keys.
Separate handheld screens allow players to occupy different nearby spaces, while choke points and multi-person mechanisms repeatedly draw the team back together.

Player count, synchronized presence, carried tools, rupee performance, area completion, and earned keys gate progress; replay supports higher collection performance rather than a solitary world revisit.
Obstacles signal required headcount, simultaneous timing, complementary positions, or a swappable tool; the solution often exists in communication between differently situated players.
The adventure is a sequence of multiplayer dungeon areas with local challenge rooms, collection targets, and bosses rather than one continuous overworld-to-dungeon map.
Rupees reward route coverage and speed while hidden paths, tool opportunities, and team separation create a tension between collective clearance and individual score.
Collected rupees act as both required progress currency and comparative score; most tools are situational stage resources rather than a growing permanent solo inventory.
Top-down encounters scale around several independent attackers and can require coordinated positions or shared exploitation while allowing players to compete for nearby rewards.
The social system is primarily player-to-player rather than NPC-driven; communication, assistance, obstruction, and score comparison create the responsive relationship layer.
A concise rescue frame establishes why the hero is divided, then stage starts, team reunions, boss clears, rupee tallies, and key awards provide the main performance cadence.
Each player has a centered personal handheld view, so blocking relies on local object readability and shared choke points rather than keeping every participant inside one camera.
Simple top-down verbs, explicit player-count obstacles, immediate switch and object reactions, rupee totals, and peer demonstration let the group teach itself during play.
Every participant uses a Game Boy Advance with a private screen and compact controls; individual health, held tool, position, and collection state remain locally readable.
A mistake affects local health, position, or shared collection performance more often than erasing the whole session, but assembling the required hardware and players is a substantial access cost.
Top-down personal views permit independent movement and off-screen separation while tile-like interaction zones make synchronized switches, carries, and enemy states precise.
The original Four Swords requires two to four Game Boy Advance systems, a copy for each participant, and link cables; it has no original single-player mode.

A faithful abstraction needs deterministic multi-client state, low-latency position and switch synchronization, player-count-aware dependencies, per-player cameras and UI, stage scoring, and disconnect-safe recovery.

contributionHypotheses: A shared-clearance goal plus individual collection score can create productive cooperation-competition tension inside the same stage.
Private centered views can support parallel search if topology periodically requires explicit reunion.
Temporary per-player tool limits may turn inventory selection into negotiated team roles.
uniqueOrNonportable: The exact fourfold hero premise, named sword, villain, princess rescue frame, themed areas, keys, and rupee presentation are recognizable authored surface content and must not be reused.
Specific room layouts, boss sequences, player-carry animations, item roster, scoring values, and audiovisual identity are nonportable.
The required GBA multi-cartridge and cable arrangement is a historical product constraint, not a default recommendation for modern projects.
openQuestions: Original stage-layout variation and exact resource penalties require a primary-play trace before they support any deterministic generation claim.
The Anniversary Edition's single-player and wireless changes should be recorded as a separate variant delta, not blended into this profile.

dimensions.summary: The player is a castaway investigator whose practical search for escape gradually becomes an emotionally uncertain inquiry into the island's nature.
The cadence connects village guidance, compact overworld search, sequential dungeons, new item combinations, and instrument acquisition that advances both route and mystery.
Sword, shield, lifting, jumping, dashing, swimming, pulling, ranged tools, and musical actions are freely assigned to two action buttons, allowing deliberate combinations.
Dungeon instruments, opened passages, moved objects, completed trades, learned songs, and NPC events persist, progressively changing routes and the island's social texture.
A screen-grid island packs village, coast, forest, mountains, caves, and dungeons into short loops, while side-view passages provide discrete underground or interior links.
Strength, jumping, dashing, swimming, hook traversal, songs, and trade-chain knowledge reopen conspicuous obstacles across frequently revisited central routes.
Environmental patterns, stone messages, owl guidance, character needs, and item reactions distribute clues between rooms and across the island rather than in a quest checklist.
Eight principal dungeons use room grids, keys, maps, compasses, a major item, a boss, and an instrument reward, with later layouts demanding more cross-room state reasoning.
Detours yield shells, heart pieces, rupees, tools, trade objects, shortcuts, and character moments, often making compact familiar spaces worth rechecking.
Heart capacity, sword improvement, item capacity, rupees, ammunition, recovery resources, and a serial barter chain create overlapping growth and exchange tracks.
Top-down encounters combine enemy-facing rules with equipped tools and movement abilities; bosses reinforce observation and item use inside compact arenas.
Island residents have memorable needs and reactions, especially a barter chain in which each exchanged object changes who can help and what route information becomes available.
Dreamlike encounters, recurring owl guidance, songs, dungeon completions, and the accumulating implications of awakening pace the mystery through player-earned revelations.
Fixed top-down screens compose readable action tableaux; transitions to side-view passages, interiors, and close character moments vary rhythm and spatial interpretation.
The lost sword creates an immediate recovery goal, early characters point toward it, and new items are followed by nearby obstacles that demonstrate their action clearly.
The original Game Boy maps any two carried tools to A and B, making the subscreen an active composition interface while hearts and counters remain continuously visible.
Persistent saves protect major progress, fairies and healing locations support preparation, and dungeon return routes limit total loss while still asking the player to repeat local traversal.
Top-down exploration is punctuated by side-view platform passages, so the same equipped verbs must remain coherent across cardinal room movement and gravity-based traversal.
The original monochrome Game Boy release is single-player; a small screen, directional pad, and two face buttons make item assignment and compact room composition central.
Its model requires a dense screen graph, two-button equipment composition, mixed movement modes, persistent trades and songs, sequential instrument gates, and narrative state tied to exploration.

contributionHypotheses: A missing starting capability can make tutorial progression feel like an immediate character need rather than an abstract lesson.
A serial trade object can encode a social route through a dense world without requiring a separate quest ledger.
Repeated challenge rewards can carry narrative escalation when each one is also a component of the story's central transformation.
uniqueOrNonportable: The island, awakening premise, named characters, exact barter objects, instruments, songs, dungeon layouts, dialogue, guest-character imagery, and ending are source-specific expression.
The precise two-button item combinations and side-view obstacle sequences are evidence rather than templates for direct reuse.
openQuestions: Later variant research should keep the 1993 monochrome original distinct from the color edition and the structurally revised 2019 remake.

dimensions.summary: The player becomes an observant time-loop intervener who learns a doomed community's routines, inhabits alternate bodies, and assembles a successful sequence from repeated attempts.
Observe schedules, acquire knowledge or a persistent capability, intervene in a timed chain, accept partial success or failure, reset the three-day cycle, and execute a better route.
The prior game's movement, targeting, tools, and melodies are expanded by form-changing masks whose movement, combat, and environmental verbs differ.
Most local events, consumables, and dungeon conditions reset with the cycle while masks, major equipment, learned songs, and selected stored resources persist.
A compact town hub and four surrounding regions are spatially stable but temporally layered by moving characters, opening hours, deadlines, and event dependencies.
Progress is gated by time windows, identity, form abilities, songs, dungeon rewards, and knowledge; repeated routes shorten as the player learns prerequisites and schedules.
Puzzles include spatial mechanisms and temporal dependency chains, often asking who must be met, in which form, at what place and time, after which prior event.
Four principal dungeons use familiar room dependencies but give strong roles to transformation movement and large state changes within the bounded structure.
Masks, bottles, health pieces, songs, notebook knowledge, and character outcomes reward attention to side paths and time-specific social events.
Persistent equipment and health grow across cycles; carried rupees and common supplies reset unless deliberately converted or stored through an established persistent service.
Targeted 3D combat supports human and transformation-specific attacks, while bosses test mobility, form switching, items, and recognition of cyclic openings.
Characters follow multi-day schedules and interdependent routines; the player's interventions can temporarily change their actions even though the next reset restores the baseline.
The visible countdown and descending moon sustain pressure while intimate, often optional character episodes give each loop emotional consequence despite systemic reversal.
Lock-on and contextual framing preserve 3D readability; transformations, clock transitions, and scheduled encounters receive brief staged emphasis that marks state change.
The compulsory opening cycle teaches loss, reset, time pressure, and one transformation before the wider quest; clocks, bells, schedules, and form reactions provide ongoing feedback.
A persistent on-screen day-and-time display, quick items, mask selection, targeting, and instrument mode make temporal and form state central interface concerns.
Returning to the first dawn is both recovery and cost: it restores opportunity and world baseline but discards nonpersistent progress; original save rules demand deliberate cycle planning.
The 3D camera supports place recognition and form-specific traversal, while repeated spatial paths expose even small temporal changes in occupancy and affordance.
The original is single-player and requires the N64 Expansion Pak; analog movement, targeting, and quick buttons support rapid switching among temporal and form tools.
A faithful abstraction requires a deterministic global clock, actor schedules, event dependency graphs, reset and persistence whitelists, form-specific controllers, and recoverable loop simulation.

contributionHypotheses: A deterministic short cycle can turn character schedules into navigable level structure and player knowledge into progression.
A visible reset whitelist may support consequential experimentation without conventional irreversible branching.
Alternate bodies can unify traversal ability, combat style, and social identity in one reusable verb family.
uniqueOrNonportable: The exact three-day moon catastrophe, named town and residents, mask identities, melodies, transformation designs, and quest schedules are recognizable authored surface content and must not be reused.
Specific appointment chains, dungeon layouts, boss sequences, dialogue outcomes, iconography, and the displayed clock treatment are nonportable.
The emotional stories attached to particular masks are authored narrative expressions, not generic mechanic templates.
openQuestions: Original N64 and later 3DS save, schedule, and assistance changes should be represented as version deltas before recovery comparisons include both.
A later trace should quantify how many critical chains expose schedule information before their first miss without relying on walkthrough sources.

dimensions.summary: The player grows from an isolated child into a time-crossing hero whose competence is expressed by reading a three-dimensional world and helping its communities.
Follow a lead from the central field or a settlement, enter a bounded challenge, learn an item rule, defeat an exam encounter, and use the new capability to reinterpret earlier routes.
Analog movement, context actions, lock-on sword and shield combat, aimed tools, object handling, riding, and performed melodies share a compact action vocabulary.
Child and adult eras expose different occupants, hazards, routes, and affordances while selected quest progress and acquired capabilities persist across the transition.
A broad field works as a memorable hub whose visible exits lead to settlements and contained dungeons, with landmarks and route repetition building spatial memory.
Items, songs, age, story permissions, and learned environmental rules gate progress; shortcuts and changed-era routes make return travel materially different.
Puzzles present manipulable objects, targetable features, audio or visual cues, and item-compatible surfaces so a hypothesis can be attempted and immediately confirmed.
Room-and-key dependency graphs introduce a local rule, add navigation pressure around a central device or spatial theme, and end with an item-centered boss exam.
Optional routes yield health pieces, bottles, collectible tokens, songs, ammunition capacity, and small discoveries that expand either endurance or future possibility.
Health, magic, wallets, ammunition limits, bottles, and durable tools grow over the adventure while rupees and consumables support moment-to-moment preparation.
Lock-on stabilizes facing and camera, enabling circling, guarding, timed attacks, projectile aiming, and bosses whose tells expose a tool-mediated vulnerability.
Characters supply local goals, trade steps, songs, and state-dependent responses; day, place, and age sometimes change who is available and what can be completed.
A youthful collection quest pivots into an adult restoration journey, with era changes, location transformations, and short staged reveals carrying much of the drama.
Third-person framing, lock-on recentering, first-person aiming, entrance reveals, and selective fixed cameras keep actors, targets, and room-scale mechanisms readable.
The opening settlement isolates movement, targeting, dialogue, currency, and equipment lessons; companions, action prompts, effects, and enemy reactions continue contextual guidance.
The N64 analog stick, Z trigger, context-sensitive action button, and three quick item buttons make mode and target state visible while limiting simultaneous tool loadout.
Saving, bottled fairies, health drops, dungeon shortcuts, and persistent major acquisitions soften failure, though the original version offers limited explicit assistance.
Full 3D creates depth aiming, camera occlusion, vertical landmarks, and free approach angles, requiring targeting and contextual movement to reduce orientation burden.
The original is single-player; its design is materially shaped by one analog stick, a rear targeting trigger, and the N64 controller's separate item buttons.
A faithful abstraction needs target-aware locomotion and camera, room state graphs, era variants, persistent inventory and quest flags, melody recognition, and robust transition staging.

dimensions.summary: The player is a temporal problem-solver who restores a damaged land by learning how actions, structures, and relationships differ between past and present.
The cadence moves between information gathering, past-present comparison, puzzle-dense field and dungeon routes, new tools or harp tunes, and essence recovery.
Sword and shield actions combine with lifting, jumping, swimming, seed effects, ranged tools, object exchange, and increasingly flexible time travel through the Harp of Ages.
Past and present maintain related but different terrain, construction, and NPC states; permanent tools, tunes, essences, trades, rings, and linked secrets accumulate.
A compact screen-grid region exists in two temporal versions whose corresponding locations may differ in water, buildings, routes, settlements, and interaction opportunities.
Harp tunes, time portals, movement equipment, dungeon tools, and knowledge of cross-era causality reopen places whose useful state exists in another time.
Clues and visible differences invite players to form hypotheses about temporal cause, object state, switches, color, movement, and multi-room dependencies.
Eight principal dungeons use keyed room graphs, signature tools, multi-room state puzzles, bosses, and essence rewards, with later challenges increasing dependency density.
Temporal detours, caves, seed trees, rings, trades, companion paths, and linked secrets provide upgrades, resources, shortcuts, and new causal information.
Hearts, rupees, bombs, seeds, rings, upgrades, and collected materials support preparedness while new harp functions expand access without consuming a resource.
Combat remains top-down and tool-aware, but many bounded challenges place execution inside puzzle-like state constraints before bosses test observation and control.
Residents occupy era-specific situations, give clues and exchanges, and can respond differently as actions or information connect the two times and paired game.
The possessed oracle and disrupted history make temporal damage visible; rescues, era shifts, essence beats, and linked continuation pace the restoration narrative.
Fixed screens preserve landmarks for past-present comparison, while a time-shift effect and changed actor or terrain blocking stage the consequence in the same location.
Early portal use shows one coordinate in two eras, and each additional harp tune expands transition freedom with a visible effect and nearby route application.
Two assignable item buttons and a subscreen manage weapons, tools, seeds, rings, and the harp; stable counters keep health and expendables legible during puzzle action.
Persistent saves retain tools, tunes, essences, and key quest state, while healing and compact routes support retries; temporal puzzles must remain solvable from either recoverable state.
The 2D top-down view makes corresponding coordinates easy to compare, with some side-view passages adding gravity-based traversal under the same inventory system.
The original Game Boy Color title is single-player but shares state asynchronously with its paired adventure through passwords and limited link functions.
Its model requires paired temporal maps, cross-era state dependencies, legal transition anchors and tunes, persistent items, item assignment, linked secrets, and temporal anti-softlock tests.

contributionHypotheses: Paired temporal maps support causal reasoning when stable coordinates anchor a deliberately limited set of meaningful differences.
A world-state mechanic gains narrative force when it changes resident situations and not only traversal geometry.
Multi-room puzzles remain learnable when shared state has a consistent sensory signature and a reconstructible route between cause and effect.
uniqueOrNonportable: The named oracle, region, harp and tunes, eras, essences, characters, exact causal events, dungeon solutions, codes, linked ending, and audiovisual identity are source-specific.
Specific past-present correspondences are evidence only; adaptations must invent independent states, consequences, and geography rather than mirror the source map.
openQuestions: A variant review should verify original link-cable behavior separately from password continuity and later emulation feature loss.

dimensions.summary: The player restores a disrupted land by becoming an agile fighter and environmental operator who can deliberately replace the active season around local terrain.
The cadence alternates action-heavy field traversal, seasonal route experiments, dungeon tool acquisition, boss completion, and searches for the next essence or world connection.
Sword, shield, jumping, lifting, swimming, digging, ranged tools, seed effects, animal-companion movement, and season change form a layered top-down action vocabulary.
The chosen local season changes terrain behavior, while dungeon progress, tools, essences, companion state, rings, opened paths, and linked-game secrets persist.
A screen-based overworld is paired with a distinct subterranean region; portals, seasonal terrain, dungeons, and companion routes connect their compact graphs.
Season powers, movement tools, companion traversal, keys, and portal knowledge reopen stumps, water, vegetation, cliffs, and passages seen during earlier circulation.
Seasonal scenery visibly changes water, plants, snow, leaves, and terrain access, letting players compare states and infer which environmental rule creates a route.
Eight principal dungeons use keyed room dependencies, a major item, action and traversal tests, a boss, and an essence reward that advances the restoration sequence.
Optional screens, caves, seed trees, ring discoveries, companion routes, and seasonal detours provide resources, abilities, upgrades, secrets, and shortcuts.
Hearts, rupees, bombs, seeds, rings, item upgrades, and collectable materials support both immediate field decisions and long-term equipment variation.
Top-down combat emphasizes movement and tool execution, with enemy groups, hazards, action-focused dungeon rooms, and bosses testing controlled timing and positioning.
Residents supply clues, trades, services, companion relationships, and password-based secrets; their responses can unlock items or connect the paired Oracle adventures.
The abduction of the seasonal oracle makes environmental disorder visible, while essence collection, region visits, character scenes, and linked continuation pace the restoration story.
Fixed top-down screens let a seasonal transformation redraw a complete local tableau; portals and room transitions clearly separate overworld, underground, and dungeon states.
A season change immediately alters palette and terrain features around the player, while early stump placement and nearby obstacles make each acquired seasonal power testable.
Two action buttons accept items, the subscreen manages a broad tool and ring inventory, and counters communicate health, currency, bombs, and seed supplies.
Save and continue preserve campaign progress, healing and ring effects provide preparation options, and compact screen routes support local retry despite some repeated traversal.
The 2D top-down view makes before-and-after seasonal layouts directly comparable, while discrete screen states limit how much altered terrain must be presented at once.
The original Game Boy Color title is single-player but supports external cross-cartridge continuity through passwords and limited link features rather than simultaneous cooperative play.

Its principles require multi-variant tile screens, legal season-change anchors, persistent progression, portal graphs, item assignment, companion traversal, link-secret flags, and anti-softlock validation.

Stable landmarks plus changed causal terrain make multi-state comparison readable on a compact screen.
Asynchronous cross-game state can be made diegetic through character-mediated secrets.
uniqueOrNonportable: The named oracle, worlds, rod, seasonal spirits, essences, characters, exact screen variants, dungeon solutions, codes, linked ending, and audiovisual identity are source-specific.

dimensions.summary: The player is a sea-roaming rescuer and practical navigator who turns sketches, charts, and a stylus-controlled hero into progress across an unfamiliar archipelago.
The recurring cadence alternates plotting a voyage, landing on an island, solving a bounded objective, gaining a chart or capability, and returning to the sea with a wider choice of destinations.
Touching directs movement and inspection, drawn strokes produce attacks or paths, and acquired tools extend the vocabulary with ranged, digging, grappling, and context-sensitive actions.
Recovered sea charts expose new sectors, dungeon results unlock routes and tools, collected sand extends protected time, and ship equipment changes voyage resilience.
The world is a node-and-route archipelago whose islands contain compact local graphs, while a repeatedly entered central temple acts as a deepening progression spine.
Sea sectors, island obstacles, and deeper temple floors are gated by charts, tools, protected-time capacity, and learned shortcuts that make earlier spaces cheaper to traverse.
The game distributes clues across scenery, dialogue, the upper display, and maps that accept player notes, allowing remembered symbols and spatial relationships to become manipulable information.
Island dungeons provide distinct tool-and-boss arcs, while the recurring timed temple layers stealth, safe areas, keys, shortcuts, and newly reachable depths into a cumulative challenge.
Optional islands, salvaged treasure, hidden chests, ship parts, health growth, and additional sand convert curiosity into either immediate supplies or longer future expeditions.
Rupees, consumables, treasure, health, hourglass sand, and interchangeable ship parts create linked personal and vessel economies rather than a single linear power track.
Stylus attacks emphasize target position and stroke direction, while enemies and bosses ask the player to combine direct movement with a recently learned tool or arena rule.
Ciela, Linebeck, island residents, and service characters supply motives, transport, clues, trades, and optional errands that repeatedly redirect the voyage graph.
The search for the missing Tetra is staged through departures, arrivals, companion exchanges, repeated temple descents, and boss resolutions that return agency quickly to navigation.
A mostly overhead play view keeps touch targets and room causality readable, while the second screen can carry map or status information without covering the active scene.
Direct cursor-to-avatar response, visible route lines, map marks, safe-zone color, a draining time meter, and item-specific audiovisual cues expose the result of each new rule.
The stylus is the primary pointer for travel, combat, interaction, item use, route drawing, and annotation, with the two displays separating action from reference information.
Safe areas pause the central temple's time pressure, opened shortcuts reduce repeated work, and leaving to gather more sand or equipment provides a recoverable alternative to brute-force repetition.
The 2.5D overhead view maps touch coordinates directly to ground positions and preserves readable silhouettes, while sea travel shifts scale without abandoning map-led navigation.
The original uses the DS touch screen, dual displays, microphone, local wireless, download play, and online services; its separate two-player mode turns drawn paths into asymmetric pursuit control.

Reproduction requires gesture and path recognition, persistent handwritten map layers, dual-screen layouts, boat navigation, timed protected zones, companion state, and deterministic dungeon shortcuts.

contributionHypotheses: Player-authored route lines and notes can make a map an action surface rather than a passive record.
A repeatedly visited challenge space can show growth through saved time, shortcuts, and increased safe-resource capacity.
Alternating compact island objectives with plotted travel can keep a broad world legible as a node-and-route adventure.

dimensions.summary: The player is a young knight whose physical gestures, growing fieldcraft, and repeated descents from a safe sky community gradually make him capable of protecting a close friend and founding a heroic legacy.

Prepare in the sky hub, choose a surface landing, traverse a dense puzzle-field, enter its dungeon, acquire or refine a capability, return upward to upgrade and socialize, then revisit a transformed region.

Directional sword swings, thrust, shield bash, lock-on, stamina-limited sprint and climbing, sailcloth descent, dowsing, flying, aimed tools, object manipulation, and motion-shaped item use form an embodied vocabulary.

Three surface regions acquire successive route and hazard variants, local time-shift fields replace selected present states with past states, and sky-surface links open through story and activated landmarks.
Aerial Skyloft and scattered sky islands form the service hub, while separated surface regions behave like dense obstacle courses whose familiar landforms are reused under changed conditions.
Story songs, dowsing targets, stamina routes, new tools, landing statues, local state devices, and upgraded equipment gate progress; revisits often use the same terrain with a new rule or objective.
Enemy guards, directional cuts, mechanism orientation, stamina paths, dowsing signals, local temporal boundaries, and tool-reactive surfaces provide explicit hypotheses and fast feedback.
Distinct dungeons continue item-and-boss dependency structures while the surrounding surface itself adopts dungeon-like density, sequencing, and environmental rule escalation.
Insects, upgrade treasures, health pieces, goddess cubes and their sky rewards, side-quest tokens, bottles, and optional islands connect careful surface search to later hub benefits.
Collected treasures and insects support equipment upgrades; wallet, item pouch, shield durability, ammunition, potions, health, and rupees make preparation and loadout management explicit.
Enemies visibly guard directions, so sword angle, feint, distance, shield timing, stamina, and item choice matter; bosses escalate the same embodied read-and-response grammar.

The sky settlement supports day-and-night routines, favors, shops, upgrades, and relationship episodes; a companion supplies analysis and dowsing while the central rescue relationship drives the route.
Frequent cinematics, repeated near-meetings, ritual trials, region transformations, and gesture-driven confrontations alternate authored drama with player-performed action.
Targeting stabilizes combat, first-person-like dowsing and aiming isolate a search direction, flight and descent cameras expose altitude, and staged cuts emphasize character and mechanism response.

A long safe prologue teaches movement, flight, sword direction, social context, and interface; companion analysis, training rooms, optional hint media, telegraphs, and repeated landforms reinforce rules.
The Wii Remote Plus and Nunchuk are required; motion direction drives sword and several items, while radial selections, stamina, shield state, dowsing, and recalibration cues expose active mode.

Bird statues save and connect surface routes, stamina fruit restores traversal capacity, seats and consumables restore health, shields can be repaired or replaced, and optional hints reduce puzzle deadlock.

Third-person 3D accommodates directional gesture combat, altitude, dense multi-level terrain, and local temporal overlays, but the original's limited free-camera control increases dependence on targeting and authored framing.
The original is single-player and requires Wii MotionPlus capability plus a Nunchuk; physical calibration, player space, dominant-hand mapping, and gesture recognition are core design constraints.

A faithful abstraction needs directional motion classification and calibration, target-relative guard states, stamina and durability, surface-state variants, local temporal volumes, hub-field transit, dowsing signals, upgrades, and cinematic handoff.

dimensions.summary: The player acts as both field adventurer and responsible engineer, restoring a damaged rail network while coordinating closely with an embodied companion.
The cadence moves from tower guidance to route restoration, train travel, settlement or dungeon action, a returned map segment, and a larger rail network of destinations and services.
Stylus movement, attacks, tool gestures, route drawing, train speed and whistle control, microphone flute play, and directed companion paths form a hardware-shaped verb set.

New track segments, tools, songs, and companion capabilities open destinations and allow earlier routes or tower floors to be approached with broader options.
Puzzles externalize coordination through drawn companion paths, synchronized positions, item interactions, rail diagrams, audible melodies, and visible timing relationships.

dimensions.summary: The player is an improvising explorer-engineer who rebuilds capability from found matter and links sky, surface, caves, and depths into self-made routes.

The cadence is to identify a destination or need, gather nearby materials and devices, construct or fuse a solution, traverse one of the world's layers, and convert discovery into stronger future improvisation.
Climbing, gliding, fighting, cooking, and gathering return alongside Ultrahand assembly, Fuse, Recall, Ascend, device activation, blueprint recreation, throwing, and material-enhanced archery.

Built objects, moved geometry, lit roots, completed shrines and quests, cleared phenomena, map discovery, weapon fusions, environmental time reversal, and temporary devices operate at several persistence scales.
A vertically layered Hyrule connects sky islands, surface terrain, caves, wells, chasms, and the depths through falls, launches, Ascend surfaces, holes, towers, and player-built vehicles.

An opening sky island teaches core powers, while later access is often governed by knowledge, preparation, vertical entry, environmental resistance, devices, regional story state, or newly gained support.
Loose components, attachment points, device affordances, motion histories, overhead surfaces, material effects, and example constructions expose a combinatorial rule grammar.

dimensions.summary: The player inhabits a lightly directed lone adventurer who turns an initially dangerous wilderness into a personally understood route network.
The recurring cadence is to scan an overworld screen, survive its enemies, test a route or suspicious feature, and convert discoveries into deeper dungeon progress.
Movement and sword use form the base vocabulary, while bombs, flame, projectiles, pushing, rafting, and crossing gaps add context-specific world verbs.
Collected relic pieces, acquired tools, opened dungeon routes, and revealed caves persist, so exploration steadily changes what the player can reach and afford.
A fixed-screen overworld grid connects landmark-like terrain screens to hidden interiors and nine separate labyrinth graphs, rewarding remembered adjacency.
Some obstacles yield to a newly acquired item and others to knowledge about an unmarked entrance, making both capability and memory valid revisit keys.
Puzzle information is deliberately sparse: room geometry, enemy clearance, block placement, and short cave messages invite experimentation rather than explicit procedures.
Labyrinths are room graphs with locked doors, keys, maps, compasses, a major tool, a boss encounter, and an objective fragment that closes the local arc.
Curiosity can reveal currency, shops, health growth, tools, shortcuts, or required entrances, so an uncertain detour may improve either present survival or future access.
Heart capacity, sword upgrades, item capacity, keys, bombs, and rupees provide both permanent growth and expendable decision pressure.
Top-down combat asks the player to read enemy movement, approach angles, shield-facing behavior, projectile lanes, and the limited supply of secondary attacks.
NPC presence is compact and functional: cave inhabitants grant equipment, sell supplies, impose costs, or communicate terse clues that alter the player's search.
A brief premise establishes the rescue and collection goal, after which progress is performed mostly through traversal, acquisition, dungeon completion, and the final confrontation.
Each overworld or dungeon room is framed as one complete tactical tableau, and edge scrolling clearly marks the move to a neighboring stateful screen.
The visible starting cave offers an early weapon, while immediate changes in attack ability, pickups, door states, and sounds confirm learned rules with little textual instruction.
A compact status display exposes health and resources, while the subscreen selects one secondary item and shows accumulated progression information.
Failure supports continued attempts from established recovery locations, but lost time and repeated traversal preserve meaningful pressure; persistent progress prevents a total restart.
The 2D top-down view makes cardinal routes and enemy lanes readable but hides vertical layering, so caves and scrolling boundaries express depth as discrete transitions.
The original Disk System release is single-player and uses a small digital-button vocabulary plus persistent disk data rather than simultaneous or networked play.
Reproducing its principles requires deterministic room grids, screen-to-screen transitions, collision-triggered secrets, inventory gates, resource counters, and persistent per-room flags.

Knowledge gates and capability gates can coexist when new tools cause players to reinterpret previously ordinary terrain.

dimensions.summary: The player becomes a scale-shifting craft hero who notices hidden communities and transforms ordinary objects into large navigational problems by becoming tiny.
